Return Engagement For Basketball Camp
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IT is unusual in Westchester County, known for an opulent life style and expensive homes, for the affluent and the disadvantaged to interact with one another, but beginning next month, youths from well-to-do families will gather with boys and girls who have been forced to cope with illness, tragedy or broken homes. The motivation is a love of basketball. 

     Beginning on July 8, the MVP Basketball Camps, a nonprofit organization, will sponsor a three-week basketball camp at White Plains Middle School on Grandview  Avenue here. The camp's mission is to help boys and girls 5 to 16 improve their  basketball skills and develop character and self-esteem.

nytimes_articles.abstract:

     For religious reasons, she has literally kept herself under wraps. But a decree in 1994 by Egypt's Education Minister has forced girls in public schools  to bare their faces or not bother showing up.

TELEVISION VIEW;American Indians of Flesh, Blood and Hope
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IN THE POPULAR IMAGINATION, American Indians have usually been defined in the past tense. There's a museum mustiness in the treatment of native peoples in films, an approach that tends toward over-reverent, one-dimensional characterizations, predictable story lines and nostalgia for the good old days of an Eden before European contact. From "F Troop" to the more recent well-intentioned but stultifying TNT cable network movies honoring native people past and present, Hollywood Indians generally lack the vibrancy and immediacy of flesh and blood human beings. They stand for ideas rather than have them on their own. 

     "Grand Avenue," an HBO "original drama event" that has its premiere tonight at 8, does not avoid all of the genre's pitfalls, but it takes a giant step toward offering a gritty and unsparing depiction of contemporary urban Indian life. Based upon Greg Sarris's stunning 1995 collection of interrelated short stories of the same title -- and adapted by Mr. Sarris -- the three-hour presentation turns a reverse telescope on a situation that's invisible to most Americans but a reality for approximately half of this nation's 2.2 million Indians.

Developers Are Bullish On Wall Street
nytimes_articles.abstract:
WHEN the union of two pivotal engineering achievements, the elevator and the  steel frame, spawned the skyscraper at the close of the 19th century, the new form flourished in lower Manhattan. In 1911, intrigued by the potential to accommodate multiple uses in a single framework, Theodore Starrett of the Starrett real estate family proposed a 100-story tower to house industry, business, residences and a hotel in a self-contained vertical city. But the idea of living as well as working in lower Manhattan became the exception rather than the rule; the skyscrapers that were built downtown were monolithic "cathedrals of commerce." 

     Now Starrett's vision may be coming to pass. Less than a year after an incentive plan for residential conversions in lower Manhattan was approved by the State Legislature, at least 20 buildings are slated for alteration, and developers are mining the canyons of Wall Street for more.

LIVES;Disappearing Acts
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I'VE BEEN AN EXPATRIATE FOR FIVE YEARS. LIVING AN ocean away, farther than I  meant to go, longer than I meant to stay. I took off suddenly, leaving my homemade messes like Hansel and Gretel's bread crumbs. For the birds. 

     Folk tales, history, the Bible are all full of expatriates. Adam and Eve, Gertrude Stein, Grace Kelly, Dorothy and Toto. They'd be called ex-pats now, for short. Or P.T.'s (permanent travelers) if they kept moving. Jaunty post-modern nicknames for a guy like Rick running a cafe in "Casablanca." But there's no way to tempt fate anymore disguised as Dietrich on a train to Shanghai, hiding the past behind veils, forged papers and smoke. You can ride the Orient-Express, all right; but now it's just an excursion for rich tourists.

SOAPBOX;Dredge We Must. But First . . .
nytimes_articles.abstract:
PEOPLE call it a "dredging crisis." But while virtually everyone agrees that  New York Harbor must be dredged to clear the rising shoals of silt and muck that clog its shipping berths and channels, the real dilemma lies in what to do with the stuff once it is dredged. What the harbor faces is not a dredging crisis but something much more familiar to New Jerseyans: a dumping crisis. 

     And despite much noise to the contrary, Governor Whitman and George E. Pataki, her counterpart in New York, have not come close to resolving it.

Building a Shopper-Friendly South Orange, Store by Store by Store
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Once affluent and bustling, South Orange, like many small towns around the state and the nation, fell on hard times in the 1970's. Small-business owners fought hard to stay afloat while malls like those at Livingston and Short Hills lured shoppers away. By the early 90's, 26 of 130 stores had closed their doors, giving the downtown a desolate air. 

 But the village is fighting back. In September 1991, business owners and residents formed a nonprofit volunteer group, Main Street South Orange, to revitalize the downtown. Now it has two paid employees: Gabrielle McGill, a six-year resident with a background in marketing and investment research, and Cathy Faughnan, an urban planner who has lived here four years, share the job of program manager.

STYLE;Birthday Suit
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Fifty years ago this week, on the banks of the Seine in Paris, something remarkable happened. There was a swimsuit show -- normal activity for the Capital of Fashion. But halfway through, out strolled a buxom blonde in a tiny two-piece, the bottom dipping below the navel. Onlookers gasped. Reporters panted. "The bikini!" announced its inventor, Louis Reard, a French engineer, who took the name from the South Pacific atoll where the United States was testing A-bombs. 

     Imagine for a moment the social climate at the time. The war was over. The spirit of liberation was everywhere. Women were stronger, more defiant of the male-dominated society. The bikini was seen as a manifestation of this new-found freedom: it gave women the power to show off as much flesh as they wanted, to the consternation of their fathers or mates. If the casting off of corsets by suffragettes was the beginning of feminism, then the bikini launched women's sexual emancipation.

The Wilderness Just Off The Jersey Turnpike
nytimes_articles.abstract:
MOST people imagine New Jersey, the most densely populated state in the country, as veined by thousands of miles of highways knotting through factories, shopping malls, and suburbs. That image is partly accurate, but there is a wild  heart to the state that can be found in the 1.1 million acres of the Pinelands National Reserve and in particular in its carefully protected 300,000-acre preservation district, which encompasses five state forests. 

     With my 13-year-old son, Owen, last spring I exited from the traffic of the Garden State Parkway and headed for the New Jersey Pine Barrens, which stretch from Toms River in the central part of the state to the waters of the Delaware Bay that lap against the Belleplain State Forest in the south.

The 96-page book -- "Now I Know Better: Kids Tell Kids About Safety" -- is published by the Children's Hospital at Yale-New Haven. The stories were submitted by students who were asked to write about an accident that happened to them or to someone they knew.

VOICES: FROM THE DESK OF;Skill by Skill, Year by Year
nytimes_articles.abstract:
AT a recent business dinner, the man sitting next to me asked how a math major like myself ended up as a newsletter editor. When I said it was a long story, he must have assumed it was unpleasant. He politely changed the subject.  

     But the story is neither distasteful nor complicated. Three years ago, when the "too big to fail" real estate company that employed me did the unthinkable,  I was ready. Not through great planning on my part, but by following a deceptively simple piece of advice received from a friend about 15 years earlier. Every year, he said, he tried to add a skill to his repertoire, and he  urged me to do the same.

A Tale of Two Harlems
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I KNOW that the merchants of 125th Street are struggling. I see them every week through the windows of my tour bus. They're battling a two-sided negative public image. First, there's the outsider impression that Harlem is a dangerous place and not a shopper's paradise. Second, there's the residents' view that those store owners who live outside the area are rich, making money off the backs of the poor.

 But a tour guide has a unique ability to get people from all over the world to come to New York and spend money. It's my duty, as a citizen of New York, to make the whole city more attractive to others, and to get people to shop in the areas that need it the most.

Collateral Damage in War on Red Tape
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When the City Council adopted a Giuliani administration plan last fall to create a central agency responsible for city-sponsored construction projects, the decision was widely hailed as a blow to big government and costly red tape. But now, a pair of little noticed provisions in the bill that created the agency have angered some community boards.

 With the new Department of Design and Construction came a mandate that community boards reduce the review process for construction projects from a maximum of 90 days to 30 days. And for the first time since the city's 59 community boards were created in 1975, they were required to meet during the summer.

The Wilderness Just Off The Jersey Turnpike
nytimes_articles.abstract:
MOST people imagine New Jersey, the most densely populated state in the country, as veined by thousands of miles of highways knotting through factories, shopping malls, and suburbs. That image is partly accurate, but there is a wild heart to the state that can be found in the 1.1 million acres of the Pinelands National Reserve and in particular in its carefully protected 300,000-acre preservation district, which encompasses five state forests.

 With my 13-year-old son, Owen, last spring I exited from the traffic of the Garden State Parkway and headed for the New Jersey Pine Barrens, which stretch from Toms River in the central part of the state to the waters of the Delaware Bay that lap against the Belleplain State Forest in the south.

Dredge We Must. But First . . .
nytimes_articles.abstract:
PEOPLE call it a ''dredging crisis.'' But while virtually everyone agrees that New York Harbor must be dredged to clear the rising shoals of silt and muck that clog its shipping berths and channels, the real dilemma lies in what to do with the stuff once it is dredged. What the harbor faces is not a dredging crisis but something much more familiar to New Jerseyans: a dumping crisis.

 And despite much noise to the contrary, Governor Whitman and George E. Pataki, her counterpart in New York, have not come close to resolving it.

ON THE MAP;Building a Shopper-Friendly South Orange, Store by Store by Store
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Once affluent and bustling, South Orange, like many small towns around the state and the nation, fell on hard times in the 1970's. Small-business owners fought hard to stay afloat while malls like those at Livingston and Short Hills  lured shoppers away. By the early 90's, 26 of 130 stores had closed their doors, giving the downtown a desolate air. 

     But the village is fighting back. In September 1991, business owners and residents formed a nonprofit volunteer group, Main Street South Orange, to revitalize the downtown. Now it has two paid employees: Gabrielle McGill, a six-year resident with a background in marketing and investment research, and Cathy Faughnan, an urban planner who has lived here four years, share the job of program manager.

nytimes_articles.abstract:
LIPS puckered, the model in a Virginia Slims advertisement is painting her nails with a garish shade of blue. "Does this look stupid on me?" the accompanying copy asks and immediately responds: "The correct answer to the question, is 'No.' " The cigarette's new slogan? It's a woman thing. 

     This is a long way from "You've Come a Long Way, Baby," the company's infamous former tag line. But it neatly spoofs the current fascination with nail enamel made in, yes, stupid colors. The new selections include Sushi, Navel and  Porno from Hard Candy Inc. and shades called Oil Slick and Bruise from Urban Decay. And that's just the fingertip of the iceberg.

BEHIND THE WHEEL/Volkswagen Passat TDI;Knock, Knock. Who's There?
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THEY are called "oil burners," and you can hear them coming before they arrive. The frugal diesel engine, an 1892 invention of the German engineer Rudolf Diesel, has been embraced by thrifty Europeans since the first diesel passenger car, the Mercedes-Benz 260D, was introduced in 1936. 

     But the efficient, cacophonous, long-running diesel engine never really caught on in the United States, where gasoline has been cheap and plentiful. Now, after nearly disappearing from American roads, the engine is making another run at this country under the hood of the Volkswagen Passat TDI, which stands for "turbocharged direct injection." Available in Passat sedans and wagons, the  1.9-liter four-cylinder TDI engine differs in many respects from conventional diesels.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: NEW YORK UP CLOSE;Collateral Damage in War on Red Tape
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When the City Council adopted a Giuliani administration plan last fall to create a central agency responsible for city-sponsored construction projects, the decision was widely hailed as a blow to big government and costly red tape.  But now, a pair of little noticed provisions in the bill that created the agency have angered some community boards. 

     With the new Department of Design and Construction came a mandate that community boards reduce the review process for construction projects from a maximum of 90 days to 30 days. And for the first time since the city's 59 community boards were created in 1975, they were required to meet during the summer.

Americans Left Unmoved by the Diesel's Beat
nytimes_articles.abstract:
DIESEL engines have their gritty, efficient charms, but they have never managed to seduce many Americans. The nation's long love affair with smooth-running, fast-accelerating, gas-hungry V8's has given drivers little patience for the noise, fumes and sluggish performance that have generally accompanied the 104-year-old technology. 

     True, diesels power America's locomotives, passenger buses and big-rig trucks, and many of the Big Three's workhorse pickups, because of their abundant power at low engine speeds. But the diesel-powered car has gone unloved and unappreciated after briefly surging in popularity in the 1970's.

Books in Brief: FICTION;The Nanny
nytimes_articles.abstract: It is 1970, and a 20-year-old Jewish student of philosophy in Antwerp is reading obsessively, though Nietzsche and Schopenhauer hardly satisfy her. Not surprising, since Chaya is in search of a way of dealing with the horrors of the Holocaust. She blazes through books about Judaism, as well as physics and astronomy, but her most sophisticated readings are of her parents and the Kalmans, the Orthodox family for whom she works as a nanny. Chaya's mother, once "harnessed like a donkey to a cart that she had to pull along the streets of Auschwitz," now dedicates herself to baking and weaving. Chaya's father is absorbed by plans to dig up the suitcases that he buried during the war. In Jeannette K. Ringold's translation from the Dutch of Carl Friedman's novel THE SHOVEL AND THE LOOM (Persea, $20), the author perfectly accomplishes the delicate task of making unspeakable stories at once accessible and shocking. Three-year-old Simcha Kalman takes Chaya by surprise: though his life is governed by laws she mistrusts, she finds herself falling in love with him. Indeed, this devotion presents itself as a vital part of her search for meaning. Yet in the end, the author gives even this small redemption a tragic twist, as if to acknowledge the ultimate inexplicability of life.   Katherine Alberg

nytimes_articles.abstract:
IT is unusual in Westchester County, known for an opulent life style and expensive homes, for the affluent and the disadvantaged to interact with one another, but beginning next month, youths from well-to-do families will gather with boys and girls who have been forced to cope with illness, tragedy or broken homes. The motivation is a love of basketball.

 Beginning on July 8, the MVP Basketball Camps, a nonprofit organization, will sponsor a three-week basketball camp at White Plains Middle School on Grandview Avenue here. The camp's mission is to help boys and girls 5 to 16 improve their basketball skills and develop character and self-esteem.

SOAPBOX;A Tale of Two Harlems
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I KNOW that the merchants of 125th Street are struggling. I see them every week through the windows of my tour bus. They're battling a two-sided negative public image. First, there's the outsider impression that Harlem is a dangerous  place and not a shopper's paradise. Second, there's the residents' view that those store owners who live outside the area are rich, making money off the backs of the poor. 

     But a tour guide has a unique ability to get people from all over the world to come to New York and spend money. It's my duty, as a citizen of New York, to  make the whole city more attractive to others, and to get people to shop in the  areas that need it the most.

Russia Heads for Trouble
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Boris Yeltsin's recent hiring and firing of several top aides has produced a  strange euphoria among some Western experts on Russia. They predict that President Yeltsin will be re-elected in tomorrow's runoff election. They also interpret the dismissal of four hard-liners and the promotion of former Gen. Aleksandr Lebed as meaning that democracy has won out, that Mr. Lebed will be its guarantor and that economic reform can now forge ahead. 

     In my view, the most hopeful realistic scenario looks very different. Democracy is no less endangered now than it was before. Economic reform will continue to be hindered by a heavily indebted state that performs badly and strangles business activity with red tape and excessive taxes. In his new capacity as Mr. Yeltsin's top security adviser, Mr. Lebed will undoubtedly challenge rampant corruption and organized crime -- the most pernicious symptoms of state weakness. But these forces will almost certainly defeat him.
